Facts.text: Roman usurper Nepotianus of the Constantinian dynasty proclaims himself Roman Emperor and enters Rome at the head of a group of gladiators
Crusaders seize Antioch after a five-month siege during the First Crusade
French scholar Peter Abelard is found guilty of heresy
Treaty of Novgorod delineates borders between Russia and Norway in Finnmark
Peace of Ath signed (in modern Belgium), settles Brabant succession
Construction of the oldest stone church in French North America, Notre-Dame-des-Anges, begins in Quebec City, New France
Dutch West India Company receives charter for the West Indies (the Americas, Caribbean and West Africa)
Amsterdam establishes municipal postal service
Moscow houses and churches destroyed by fire
Mission San Carlos Borromeo de Carmelo founded in California
US army officially established by Congress of the Confederation
Maratha Wars between British and Maratha Confederacy in India ends
4th national black convention meets (Philadelphia)
First baseball uniforms are worn as the NY Knickerbockers don straw hats, white shirts, and blue long trousers
Cullen Whipple patents a machine for making screws
Comanche, Iowa, completely destroyed by one of a series of tornadoes
1st American Civil War land battle: Union forces defeat the Confederacy at Philippi in modern-day West Virginia
Lacrosse introduced in Britain and Canada
John Lynch (R-MS) chosen 1st black major-party national convention chair
24 Christians burn to death in Namgongo, Uganda
Baseball poem "Casey at the Bat" is first published by the San Francisco Examiner
The Canadian Pacific Railway is completed from coast to coast
Liverpool Football Club is founded
Centro Escolar University is established by Librada Avelino and Carmen de Luna in Manila, Philippines
"Come Josephine in My Flying Machine" record by Fred Fisher and Alfred Bryan, sung by Ada Jones and Billy M somgwriters urray hits #1
Dutch 1st Chamber accepts Health laws
Austro-German forces recapture Przemysl, a crucial city in southeastern Poland, and the entire Russian front begins to collapse
US National Defense Act establishes Reserve Officers Training Corps
Boston Red Sox pitcher Dutch Leonard throws his 2nd no-hitter; beats Detroit Tigers, 5-0 at Navin Field
US Supreme Court in Hammer v. Dagenhart rules child labor laws unconstitutional
Supreme Liberty Life Insurance Co (Chicago) - first insurance company organized by African Americans formed
A sudden cloudburst kills 120 near Pikes Peak, Colorado
Gila Wilderness Area established by US Forest Service
Goodyear's first advertising airship, later named "Pilgrim" takes flight over Akron, Ohio; it is the first to fly using helium, and first to have an enclosed cabin
1st trade show at Atlantic City Convention Center (electric light)
Chile and Peru sign the Treaty of Lima, finally resolving their border dispute from the War of the Pacific (1879–83). Chile keeps Arica and Peru regains Tacna.
Grover Cleveland Alexanderis released by the Phillies
John McGraw, who came to NY in 1902, resigns as manager of Giants
A's score 11 runs in 2nd, Yanks score 10 in 5th & win 17-11
French liner SS Normandie sets Atlantic crossing record of four days, three hours and 14 minutes on her maiden voyage
One thousand unemployed Canadian workers board freight cars in Vancouver, British Columbia, beginning a protest trek to Ottawa, Ontario
German law on "Entartete Art" legalizes art robbery
"Beer Barrel Polka" by Will Glahe hits #1 on the pop singles chart
Attack on telephone exchange in Schiphol
German occupiers stamp "J" on Jewish passports
United Nations Relief & Rehabilitation Administration forms
Generals Giraud & de Gaulle reach agreement on constitution
Nazis pull out of Rome
1st bikini bathing suit displayed in Paris
International Military Tribunal opens in Tokyo against 28 Japanese war criminals
US Supreme Court rules  that segregating riders by race on interstate buses violates the Interstate Commerce Clause of the US Constitution (Morgan v. Virginia)
200" (5.08 m) Hale telescope dedicated at Palomar Observatory
Korczak Ziolkowski begins sculpture of Crazy Horse near Mt Rushmore
1st African American to graduate from US Naval Academy (Wesley Anthony Brown)
Dragnet is 1st broadcast on radio (KFI in Los Angeles)
Georgia Neese Clark Gray becomes the 1st female Treasurer of the United States
French expedition reaches top of Himalayan peak of Annapurna in Nepal
Romanian communist leader Petru Groza chosen as President of the Presidium of the Great National Assembly
KVOS TV channel 12 in Bellingham/Vancouver, WA (CBS) begins
KLFY TV channel 10 in Lafayette, LA (CBS) begins broadcasting
KGUN TV channel 9 in Tucson, AZ (ABC) begins broadcasting
Third-class travel on British Railways ends
Referendum allows city to sell Chavez Ravine to the Dodgers
1st US Air Force Academy graduation in Colorado Springs, Colorado
Singapore adopts constitution
Air France Boeing 707 crashes on takeoff from Paris, kills 130
WBKO TV channel 13 in Bowling Green, KY (ABC) begins broadcasting
A Northwest Airlines DC-7 crashes in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of British Columbia, killing 101
1st US Space walk made by NASA astronaut Ed White during the Gemini 4 mission (23 minutes)
Gemini 4 launches; second US two-man flight (McDivitt and White); mission includes first US spacewalk
European DX Council forms in Copenhagen (shortwave listeners)
Gemini 9 launched; 7th US 2-man flight (Stafford & Cernan)
Canada announces it will replace silver with nickel in coins
Poor Peoples March on Washington, D.C.
Yanks turn 21st triple-play in their history lose 4-3 to Twins
Last episode of Star Trek airs on NBC (Turnabout Intruder)
First artificial gene is synthesized
"Hot Rod Lincoln" by Commander Cody & His Lost Planet Airmen hits #9
First female US rabbi is installed: 25-year-old Sally J. Priesand
At Paris air show, Tupolev 144, a Soviet supersonic airliner ("Concorde-ski"), crashes, 15 killed
Queen's "Bohemian Rhapsody" goes gold
Test Cricket debut of Mike Brearley v West Indies (0 & 17)
Baltimore Orioles pull their 6th triple play (9-6-4-6-6 vs KC Royals)
Belgium government of Tindemans forms
US & Cuba talk about diplomatic relations
Phillies Dave Johnson is 1st to hit 2 pinch hit grand slams in a year
33rd Tony Awards: "The Elephant Man" (play) and "Sweeny Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street" (musical) win
Ixtoc I rig in the Gulf of Mexico blows out, spilling 3 million barrels of oil in one of the worst oil spills in history
ESPN begins televising college world series games
Mexican architect Luis Barragán is awarded architecture's Pritzker Prize at a ceremony at Dumbarton Oaks, Washington D.C.
55th National Spelling Bee: Molly Dieveney wins spelling psoriasis

Israeli Ambassador to the UK Shlomo Argov seriously wounded in assassination attempt by members of the Abu Nidal Organization, a Palestinian militant group, outside of the Dorchester Hotel in London England
Prolific studio drummer Jim Gordon murders his mother, Osa Marie Gordon in North Hollywood, California.
38th Tony Awards: "The Real Thing" (play) & "La Cage aux Folles" (musical) win
Brewers draft B J Surhoff #1
Massive anti-ETA demonstration in Basques
Battles in Beirut; 53 killed
"Little Shop of Horrors" released in France
Cubs & Astro tie Oriole & Ranger record of 3 grand slams in a game
Houston Astros beat LA Dodgers, 5-4, in 22 innings (7:14:09)
Leaking pipe in Asha, USSR, causes 2 trains to catch fire; 460 die
3rd Children's Miracle Network Telethon raises $894,560
44th Tony Awards: "The Grapes of Wrath" (play) and "City of Angels" (musical) win
Kuwait asks Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) members to produce 800,000 bbl/d (130,000 m3/d) of oil on its behalf
MLB Draft: East Carteret HS pitcher Brien Taylor first pick by New York Yankees
Mount Unzen erupts in Japan, worst eruption in Japanese history
Joan Lunden ordered to pay her ex-husband $18,000 a month support
Landmark Australian High Court Mabo Decision: recognizes Torres Strait Islanders ownership of Murray Island, rendering terra nullius a legal fiction
World's largest environmental summit opens in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
66th National Spelling Bee: Geoff Hooper wins spelling kamikaze
5.9 earthquake/floods SE Java (150+ killed)
WIIZ (98.7) FM goes off the air
"Party Girl", directed by Daisy von Scherler Mayer and starring Parker Posey, premieres online. It is the first feature film to premiere on the internet.
1st Blockbuster Entertainment Awards held at Pantages Theatre, Hollywood
Eschede train disaster: an ICE high speed train derails in Lower Saxony, Germany, causing 101 deaths
Iraq announces that it will halt crude oil exports in response to the UN's resolution that extends the oil-for-food program by only 1 month, instead of the normal 6-month period
The union of Serbia and Montenegro comes to an end with Montenegro's formal declaration of independence
USS Carter Hall (LSD-50) engages with pirates after they board Danish ship Danica White off the coast of Somalia
Plane crash in Lagos, Nigeria, kills all 152 passengers and 40 people on the ground
Suicide car bombing in Bauchi, Nigeria, kills 15 people and injures 42
119 people are killed in a poultry farm fire in Jilin Province, China
20 people, including 10 children, are killed by a suicide bombing in Kabul, Afghanistan
US extends sanctions against Iran through its automotive industry and currency
Hashim Amla becomes the first non-white captain of the South African national cricket team
200 people are killed by an explosion at a gasoline station in Accra, Ghana
Dr. Jesse Selber performs the world's first partial-skull and scalp transplant at Houston Methodist Hospital
A week of heavy rains in Germany and France leave 10 dead and closing Paris museums along the Seine, including the Louve
Concord Records releases "Stranger to Stranger", the thirteenth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon
Rock climber Alex Honnold makes first-ever free solo climb of El Capitan, Yosemite National Park (depicted in "Free Solo" Academy Award-winning Best Documentary 2018)
Terrorist attack in Borough Market, London by three men who drive van into pedestrians then stab and kill 7 and wound 48. Attackers shot dead by British police.
Boat capsizes off eastern Tunisia drowning at least 48 migrants
Dead whale found with 17 pounds (80 pieces) of plastic in its stomach in Songkhla province, Thailand
Guatemala's Fuego volcano erupts killing at least 110, with 332 missing and forcing the evacuation of over 3,100
Apple announces it is shutting down iTunes and replacing it with three different apps
Canadian government inquiry find deaths of over 1,000 indigenous women and girls over decades who have been murdered or are missing a "national genocide"
MLB Draft: Oregon State catcher Adley Rutschman first pick by Baltimore Orioles
Sudanese military attacks protesters in Khartoum killing 100 people, some dumped in the river Nile, prompting international condemnation
US federal government departments and Congress begin anti-trust investigations into large tech companies including Apple, Google, Facebook and Amazon

Former Defense Secretary James Mattis says in The Atlantic: "Donald Trumpis the first president in my lifetime who does not try to unite the American people — does not even pretend to try. Instead, he tries to divide us."
Devon Conway becomes 7th batsman in cricket history to score a double century in his Test debut; run out for 200 for New Zealand in 1st Test against England at Lord's
NASA administrator Bill Nelson announces two new missions to study ‘Lost Habitable’ world of Venus in 2028 and 2030
NASA launches 128 baby squid and 5,000 microscopic animals, to study effects of spaceflight, to the International Space Station aboard Space X's Falcon 9 rocket
Labor unions go on strike in Nigeria taking down the country's power grid and closing airports, protest for an increase in the minimum wage from US$20 a month
Lee Jae-Myung of the Democratic Party is elected the 14th president of South Korea in snap election
Henry IVof Germany storms Rome and captures St. Peter's Basilica
Spanish conquistadorHernando de Sotocrosses the Appalachian Mountains, 1st European to do so
PopeAlexander VIIappoints François de Laval vicar apostolic in New France
Duke of York and the British fleet defeat the Dutch off the coast of Lowestoft, 1st fleet action of the Second Dutch War
Jack Jouett rides to warnThomas Jeffersonof British attack
ExplorerAlexander Mackenziesets out on his first expedition to the Pacific from Fort Chipewyan (finds the Arctic Ocean instead)
GeneralRobert E. Leewins his last victory of the US Civil War at the Battle of Cold Harbor
Jesse James& his gang robs Obocock Bank (Corydon Iowa), of $15,000
British naval officerDavid Beattyis seconded to the Egyptian government and appointed second in command of the river flotilla
W. G. Grace's last day of Test cricket at age 50 years and 320 days
Belgian KingLeopold IIclaims Congo as his private possession
MLB Chicago White Sox baseball player-managerEddie Collinsbecomes the 6th to collect 3,000 hits
Future Baseball Hall of Fame first basemanLou Gehrigfirst to hit 4 consecutive HRs in a MLB game; NY Yankees beat Philadelphia A's, 20-13 at Shibe Park
German PresidentPaul von Hindenburgdisbands Heinrich Brüning's Parliament
PopePius XIencyclical "On oppression of the Church in Spain"
Josh Gibsonhits a home run just 2 feet below the rim of Yankee Stadium (580' drive)
British Viceroy of IndiaLord Mountbattenvisits Pakistan
Stan Musialhits his 300th HR
US PresidentEisenhowerroutes Canadian Prime MinisterDiefenbakera message off the Moon
American PresidentJFKand Soviet PremierKhrushchevmeet in Vienna
Lee Harvey Oswaldarrives by train in Oldenzaal, Netherlands
Ringo Starrcollapses from tonsillitis and pharyngitis, just before the Beatles embark on a World Tour
Aretha Franklin's cover of theOtis Reddingsong "Respect" reaches #1

American radical feminist Valerie Solanas attempts to assassinateAndy Warholby shooting him three times. She is later diagnosed with paranoid schizophrenia and pleads guilty to "reckless assault with intent to harm," serving a 3-year sentence.
Innovative and much loved children's picture book "The Very Hungry Caterpillar" byEric Carleis published
Ray Daviesof The Kinks travels round trip NY-London to change 1 word in "Lola," (Coca-Cola to Cherry Cola) because of BBC commercial reference ban
MLB Chicago Cubs pitcherKen Holtzman2nd no-hitter beats Cincinnati Reds, 1-0, on the road at Riverfront Stadium
Test Cricket debut ofImran Khan, v Englandl at Edgbaston (5, 0-36, 0-19)
Yitzhak Rabinreplaces resigning Israeli Prime MinisterGolda Meir, and forms a new government

Island Records releases "Exodus", the ninth studio album byBob Marley& the Wailers; featuring the hits, 'Jamming', 'Waiting In Vain', 'Three Little Birds' and 'One Love', it is named the best album of the century by Time Magazine in 1999
Crew of Soyuz 36,Valery Kubasovand Bertalan Farkas return to Earth aboard Soyuz 35
Jimmy Carterwins enough delegates for re-nomination as US President
NY Mets draftDarryl Strawberry, 18, #1
PopeJohn Paul IIreleased from hospital after assassination attempt
"Larry KingLive" debuts on CNN, airing each weeknight through December, 2010
"Big," a film directed byPenny Marshalland starringTom Hanks, premieres in the US
Margo Adams sues Boston Red Sox 3rd basemanWade Boggsfor palimony
Nolan Ryanpitches his 2nd one-hitter this season & 11th overall
Sportscasting legendVin Scullybroadcasts 23 innings in two different cities on one day
Thomas Hearnscaptures WBA light heavyweight title
"MTV Unplugged" broadcasts singer-songwriterPaul Simonand 11 of his band members performing in Queens, NYC
Expos pitcher Pedro Martinez perfect game is broken up in 10th inning as San Diego's Bip Roberts leads off with a double, Mont wins 1-0
55th Tony Awards: "The Producers" (musical) and "Proof" (play) win
TV drama "Six Feet Under" created by Alan Ball, starring Peter Krause, Michael C. Hall and Frances Conroy premieres on HBO

"The Party at the Palace" concert commemorating Queen Elizabeth's Golden Jubilee held at Buckingham Palace Garden in London; performers include: Queen,Annie Lennox,Tony Bennett,Tom Jones; The Corrs;Ozzy Osbourne,Brian Wilson, andPaul McCartney, among others
Danielle Steel’s novel “Sunset in St. Tropez” is published
"The Knight of Sainte-Hermine" byAlexandre Dumasis published in France by Editions Phébus, completed by Claude Schopp, 135 years after the author's death.
Canadian sportscasterRon MacLeansaves a man from drowning in the Delaware River in Philadelphia
New Zealand teenage singer-songwriterLordereleases her debut single "Royals" (Grammy Song of the Year, 2014)
Dove Cameronreleases single "Count Me In"
US PresidentBarack Obamaannounces his plan for a $1 billion fund to increase deployment of US troops to Europe
Largest-ever exhibition of works byPiet Mondrianopens at the Gemeentemuseum in The Hague, Netherlands to mark centenary of De Stijl movement
The Amazing World ofDr. SeussMuseum opens in Springfield, Massachusetts
HBO drama series "Succession" starringBrian Cox,  Jeremy Strong, Sarah Snook and Kieran Culkin debuts
Jay-Znamed the world's first billionaire rapper by Forbes magazine
US PresidentDonald Trumpbegins a three-day visit to the UK by calling London MayorSadiq Khan"a stone cold loser" after Khan called Trump's language that of a 20th century fascist
Three former police officers charged in connection with death ofGeorge Floydin Minneapolis, Derek Chauvin's charge upgraded to second degree murder
Ed Sheeranconcert with 77,900 concertgoers breaks attendance record at Lincoln Financial Field in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Spanish conquistadorHernando De Sotoclaims Florida for Spain
Last British and French troops evacuated from Dunkirk
A mob of 60 from the Los Angeles Naval Reserve Armory beats up everyone perceived to be Hispanic, starting the week-long Zoot Suit Riots
US presented with oldest known copy of Magna Carta
Beginning of the Tiananmen Square Massacre as Chinese troops open fire on pro-democracy supporters in Beijing
Charles II Archduke of Austria, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1590)
Jacob Neefs, Flemish engraver and publisher (worked on engravings for major artists including Rubens and van Dyck), born in Antwerp (d. after 1660) baptism date
Manuel de Egues, Spanish composer, born in San Martin del Rio, Spain (d. 1729)
David Gregory, Scottish mathematician and astronomer (Astronomiae, physicae & geometricae elementa), born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1708)
Johannes Schenck, Dutch born composer, baptized in Amsterdam, The Netherlands (d. c. 1712)
Cornelis Hop, Mayor of Amsterdam and diplomat, born in Amsterdam (d. 1762)
Giovanni Antonio Scopoli, Italian-Austrian physician and naturalist interested in entomology (Entomologia Carniolica), born in Cavalese, Italy (d. 1788)
John Francis Edward Acton, French-born English Naval Commander and PM of Naples (remembered for his brutality towards French supporters), born in Besançon, France (d. 1811)
Johann Christoph Oley, German composer, baptized in Bernburg, Saxony-Anhalt (d. 1789)
James Hook, British composer, born in Norwich, England (d. 1827)
Frédéric Thiéme, French composer, born in Reims, France (d. 1802)
Henry Shrapnel, English Army officer and inventor (created the shrapnel shell), born in Wiltshire, England (d. 1842)

Charles Bernard Desormes, French physicist and chemist who determined the ratio of the specific heats of gases and the composition of carbon monoxide and carbon disulfide, born in Dijon, France (d. 1862)
Sydney Smith, English preacher, reformer and author, born in Woodford, Essex (d. 1845)
Caspar Georg Carl Reinwardt, German-Dutch biologist (Bogor Botanical Garden, Indonesia), born in Lüttringhausen (d. 1854)
Michael Gottard Fischer, German composer and organist, born in Alach, Duchy of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1829)
William Hone, English author and bookseller (won landmark freedom of the press case against the government), born in Bath, England (d. 1842)
Charles Waterton, English naturalist and pioneering conservationist, born in Wakefield, England (d. 1865)
František Jan Škroup, Czech composer (Kde domov můj? - Czech national anthem), born in Osice, Czech Republic (d. 1862)
Richard Cobden, English politician and manufacturer (founder of the Anti-Corn-Law League), born in Dunford, England (d. 1865)
James Wilson, British-Indian economist who delivered India's first budget and introduced taxation, born in Hawick, Roxburgshire (d. 1860)
Pieter Mijer, Dutch Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies (1866-72), born in Batavia, Dutch East Indies (d. 1881)
Martin Edward Green, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army who was part of the Vicksburg Campaign, born in Fauquier County, Virginia (d. 1863)
Louis Faidherbe, French general and colonel administrator in Senegal, born in Lille, France (d. 1889)
Johan Jongkind, Dutch-French painter and printer (Marine Scenes), born in Lattrop, Netherlands (d. 1891)
Thomas Ball, American sculptor, painter and musician, born in Charlestown, Massachusetts (d. 1911)
Edward L. Youmans, American scientific writer and founder of "Popular Science" magazine, born in Coeymans, New York (d. 1887)
Charles Kinnaird Graham, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1889)
Jose Inzenga y Castellanos, Spanish composer, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1891)
Alfonse Charles Renaud de Vilback, French organist and composer, born in Montpelier, France (d. 1884)
Otho French Strahl, American Confederate Brigadier General during the Civil War, born in Morgan County, Ohio (d. 1864)[1][2]
Charles Lecocq, French composer, chiefly of operettas and comic operas (La fille de Madame Angot), born in Paris, France (d. 1918)
Paul Lindau, German playwright and critic (Der Herr im Hause), born in Magdeburg  (d. 1919)
Eugeen van Oye, Flemish writer and poet (Morning Twilight), born in Torhout, Flanders (d. 1926)
Eduardo Caudella, Romanian composer, born in Iași, Moldavia (d. 1924)
Frederik VIII, King of Denmark (1906-12), born in Yellow Palace, Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1912)
Detlev von Liliencron, German writer (Rides of the Adjutant and Other Poems), born in Kiel, Holstein (d. 1909)
Emile Paladilhe, French composer, born in Montpellier, France (d. 1926)
Garret Hobart, American politician (R) 24th US Vice President (1897-99), born in Long Branch, New Jersey (d. 1899)
Flinders Petrie, English Egyptologist (Merneptah Stele, measured the pyramids), born in London, England (d. 1942)
Ransom Eli Olds, American auto (Oldsmobile) & truck (REO) manufacturer, born in Geneva, Ohio (d. 1950)
George Howells Broadhurst, British-American playwright, director and producer responsible for opening Broadway's Broadhurst Theatre, born in Walsall, England (d. 1952)
(Ivar) Henning Mankell, Swedish pianist and composer, born in Härnösand, Sweden (d. 1930)
Aristides Agramonte y Simoni, Cuban-American Physician, Pathologist and Bacteriologist (yellow fever), born in Camagüey, Cuba (d. 1931)
Prince Baudouin of Belgium, Count of Flaanders, born in Palais de la Régence, Brussels, Belgium (d. 1891)
Otto Loewi, German physician (Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine 1936), born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany (d. 1961)
Raoul Dufy, French Fauvist painter (Palm), born in Le Havre, France (d. 1953)
Pieter Jakob Albert Adriani, Dutch tax law expert and first director of the International Tax Documentation Bureau, born in Warmenhuizen, Netherlands (d. 1974)[1]
Raymond Pearl, American biologist, statistician, and one of the founders of biometry, born in Farmington, New Hampshire (d. 1940)[1]
Mikhail Larionov, Russian avant-garde painter who co-founded rayonism (Glass, Red Rayonism), born in Tiraspol, Russian Empire (d. 1964)[1]
Emil Axman, Czech composer and musicologist (Moravian folk songs), born in Rataje, Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1949)
Roland Hayes, American lyric tenor, arranger, and composer, born in Curryville, Georgia (d. 1977)
Tom Brown, American dixieland jazz trombonist, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1958)
Mary Browne, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1912-14), born in Ventura County, California (d. 1971)
Asen Karastoyanov, Bulgarian composer, born in Samokov, Bulgaria (d. 1976)
Kavalam Madhava Panikkar, Indian statesman and diplomat (Asia & Western Dominance), born in Travancore, British India (d. 1963)
Zoltan Korda, Hungarian-British film director (Jungle Book, 4 Feathers), born in Pusztatúrpásztó, Austria-Hungary (d. 1961)
Memphis Minnie [Lizzie Douglas], American blues guitarist and singer-songwriter ("Bumble Bee"; "Me and My Chauffeur Blues"), born in Algiers, Louisiana (d. 1973)
Rosa Chacel, spanish novelist, born in Valladolid, Spain (d. 1994)
Georg von Bekesy, Hungarian-American physician and physiologist (Nobel Prize in Physioloy or Medicine 1961 for work on the inner ear), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1972)
Gerard den Brabander [Jan Gerardus Jofriet], Dutch poet (Three on One Platform, The Hollow Man), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1968)[1]
Maurice Evans, British actor (Planet of the Apes, Bewitched), born in Dorchester, Dorset, England (d. 1989)
Edward Wayne, English physician (Queen of Scotland, Wayne score), born in Leeds, England (d. 1990)
Jan Peerce [Jacob Pincus Perelmuth], American tenor (NBC Symphony, 1938-55; NY Metropolitan Opera, 1941-67; "Bluebird of Happiness"), born in New York City (d. 1984)
Martin Gottfried Weiss, German commandant of the Dachau concentration camp known as "The Demon of Dachau", born in Weiden in der Oberpfalz, Germany (d. 1946, by execution)
Norman Gallichan, New Zealand cricket all-rounder (1 Test, HS 30, 3 wickets; Manawatu, Wellington), born in Palmerston North, New Zealand (d. 1969)
Walter Robins, English cricketer (dynamic England leg-spin all-rounder), born in Stafford, England (d. 1968)
Antonio Emmanvilovich Spadavecchia, Russian composer, born in Odessa (d. 1988)
Paul Rotha, English documentary filmmaker (The World Is Rich; Cradle of Genius), born in London (d. 1984)
Adele Dixon, American actress (Calling the Tune), born in London, England (d. 1992)
Ernst vom Rath, German Nazi diplomat whose death provided the pretext for Kristallnacht, born in Frankfurt am Main, German Empire (d. 1938)
Paulette Goddard [Marion Levy], American actress (Great Dictator), born in Whitestone Landing, Long Island (d. 1990)
Wilfred Thesiger, English explorer and travel writer (Arabian Sands), born in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia (d. 2003)
Ellen Corby [Hansen], American actress (The Waltons, Vertigo, Caged), born in Racine, Wisconsin (d. 1999)
Mason Gross, American TV quiz show personality and professor (Think Fast, Two for the Money), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1977)
Richard D'Aeth, British educationist (Hughes Hall Cambridge), born in Vancouver, British Columbia (d. 2008)
William Douglas-Home, British playwright (Now .. Barabbas), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1992)
Pedro Mir, Dominican poet laureate (There Is a Country in the World, To Julia with No Tears), born in San Pedro de Macorís, Dominican Republic (d. 2000)[1]
(Ronald George) "Tim" Woodman, British WWII RAF pilot and squadron leader, born in Trowbridge, Wiltshire, England (d. 1996)
Roy Glenn, American radio and screen character actor (Rocky Jordan; The Jack Benny Show; Amos 'n' Andy; Guess Who's Coming to Dinner), born in Pittsburg, Kansas (d. 1971)
Iris Meredith [Shunn], American actress (Son of Davy Crockett, The Mystery of the Hooded Horsemen), born in Sioux City, Iowa (d. 1980)
Milton Cato, 1st Prime Minister of San Vincent and the Grenadines (1979-84), born in Saint Vincent, British Windward Islands (d. 1997)
(Edwin) "Buster" Pickens. American blues pianist (Texas Alexander,  Lightnin' Hopkins), born in Hempstead, Texas (d. 1964)
Leo Gorcey, American stage and screen comic actor, and malapropist (The Dead End Kids; The East Side Boys; The Bowery Boys), born in New York City (d. 1969)
Carl Pruitt, American jazz and blues session double-bassist, born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 1977)
Lili St. Cyr [Marie Van Schaack], American striptease performer, born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1999)
Patrick Cargill, British actor (No Wreath for the General; Hammerhead; Help!), born in London, England (d. 1996)
Henry Brandon [Lord Brandon of Oakbrook], British judge (Lord of Appeal - Ordinary) and probable spy  (Military Cross), born in Sussex (d. 1999)
Aubrey Frank, British jazz saxophonist, and educator, born in East End of London (d. 1993)
Forbes Carlile, Australian athlete and influential swimming coach, born in Melbourne (d. 2016)
Alain Resnais, French director (Muriel, Hiroshima mon amour), born in Brittany, France (d. 2014)
Iván Patachich, Hungarian contemporary classical and electro-acoustic composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1993)
Al Harewood, American jazz drummer (Grant Green, Stanley Turrentine), and teacher, born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2014)
Igor Shafarevich, Russian mathematician, born in Zhitomir, Ukraine (d. 2017)
Michael Jaffe, English professor and museum director (Fitzwilliam Museum), born in London, England (d. 1997)
Colleen Dewhurst, Canadian-American Tony and Emmy Award-winning stage and screen actress (The Blue and the Grey - "Maggie"; Anne of Green Gables), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1991)
David Holloway, British literary editor of The Daily Telegraph (1968-88) and Booker of Bookers Prize judge (1994), born in United Kingdom (d. 1995)[1][2]
Jimmy Rogers, American blues musician (Muddy Waters' Band), born in Ruleville, Mississippi (d. 1997)
Michael Gow, British army officer (head of British Army of the Rhine), born in Sheffield, England (d. 2013)
Gerhard Zwerenz, German writer and politician, born in Gablenz, Germany (d. 2015)
Thomas Winning, Scottish controversial Roman Catholic Cardinal, born in Wishaw, Scotland (d. 2001)
Carlos Veerhoff, Argentine-born German composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2011)
Hamilton Fish IV, American politician (Rep-R-NY, 1969-95), born in Washington D.C. (d. 1996)
Janez Matičič, Slovenian pianist. conductor, and pedagogue, born in Ljubljana, Yugoslavia (d. 2022)
Roscoe Bartlett, American politician (Rep-R-Maryland, 1993-2013), born in Moorland, Kentucky
